content.problemStatement:
You have with you four positive integers X,Y,Z,X, Y, Z,X,Y,Z, and CCC.

You'd like to make XXX equal to YYY. To achieve that, you can perform the following types of operations:


Pay one coin, and either increase or decrease ZZZ by 111.

The decrease operation can only be done if Z>1Z \gt 1Z>1 initially, i.e. it's not allowed to make ZZZ reach 000.
Pay CCC coins, and do one of the following:

Replace XXX with gcd⁡(X,Z)\gcd(X, Z)gcd(X,Z) and YYY with lcm(Y,Z)\text{lcm}(Y, Z)lcm(Y,Z), or
Replace XXX with lcm(X,Z)\text{lcm}(X, Z)lcm(X,Z) and YYY with gcd⁡(Y,Z)\gcd(Y, Z)gcd(Y,Z).


The operations can each be performed however many times you like, and in any order.

Find the minimum number of coins you need to pay to make XXX and YYY equal.

It can be proved that it's always possible to make them equal.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing four space-separated integers X,Y,Z,X, Y, Z,X,Y,Z, and CCC.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of coins you need to pay to make XXX and YYY equal.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: One optimal sequence of operations is:

Spend one coin to reduce ZZZ to 333.
Spend C=2C = 2C=2 coins to perform the operation X←gcd⁡(X,Z)X \gets \gcd(X, Z)X←gcd(X,Z) and Y←lcm(Y,Z)Y \gets \text{lcm}(Y, Z)Y←lcm(Y,Z).
This will turn XXX into gcd⁡(9,3)=3\gcd(9, 3) = 3gcd(9,3)=3 and YYY into lcm(3,3)=3\text{lcm}(3, 3) = 3lcm(3,3)=3, so we're done.

Test case 222: XXX and YYY are already equal, so we don't have to do anything.
